channel.title: Backups on Fernando Simões
channel.description: Recent content in Backups on Fernando Simões

item.title: The nerdy cool stuff
item.description: Being a software developer is a really hard task. We all know this. When you are a self-taught developer, it’s even harder—not because you didn’t have formal education, but because you don’t even know what the best formal education is all about. You learn some Python or JavaScript and start scripting, building a thing or two, following traces of errors and fixing them, searching for solutions online. But there’s a potential prejudicial effect in learning this way: you think that software engineering is all about doing.

item.title: Sockets, select and IO Multiplexing
item.description: IO multiplexing is a complex topic at first. But it’s the basis of “concurrency” without multiple threads or processors. So it’s pretty handy. One example of a nice use of IO multiplexing is Redis; another is Nginx. The secret of IO multiplexing is being able to handle multiple things at once. And by “things” I mean multiple behaviors that have an input or output in file descriptors. Multiplexing is a well-known resource to deal with the “we need to gather some things, and then deliver them to their own proprietors” scenario.

item.title: Playing around with fork()
item.description: The way Unix systems create a process is really not intuitive. At first, I would imagine that creating a process in the OS would be as simple as calling a systemcall create, or something like that. But, in reality, the genesis of a process creation in Unix involves creating a hierarquical tree of processes. The running program you are using to create a new process is a process itself, so the way you can create a new process is by creating a child of the current process.
